mowers.tagline: Premium roller petrol with Versamow & Roto-Stop
pros: True Versamow mulch-and-collect on the fly
Holds resale value better than any rival
cons: Versamow lever can stiffen with age
mowers.verdict: The benchmark petrol roller and the mower most lawn-care professionals secretly own. The Honda engine is the reason — it will outlast three cheaper machines and resale stays strong because everyone knows it. Worth the premium new and easily worth the premium used.
mowers.tip: Used HRX 476s rarely drop below £450 — anything under £400 with a clean deck is a buy. Check the Roto-Stop blade-brake clutch engages cleanly (it should freewheel when released). Look at the rear roller bearings for play. If the seller says "needs a service," knock £80 off — that is fair. Avoid any with welded deck repairs.

mowers.tagline: Mid-range Honda petrol with Izy reliability
pros: Honda OHC engine — reliable and easy to start
cons: Drive cable can stretch with age
mowers.verdict: The default "I want a Honda but the HRX is too much" choice. Same engine pedigree, simpler deck, much better value used. If stripes do not matter to you, this is the smarter buy.
mowers.tip: £300–380 is the fair zone for a clean used HRG 466. Test the self-propel on an incline — slipping drive belt is the most common fault. Pull start should fire on the first or second pull when warm. Check oil colour through the dipstick — golden good, black means no service history.

pros: Best-value self-propelled petrol on the UK market
Stiga engine is reliable and parts are everywhere
Light at 27kg — easy to push and store
Huge used supply keeps prices low
cons: Plastic deck not as durable as steel
Cable-operated drive less smooth than Honda
Rear flap hinge a known weak point
mowers.verdict: The mower nearly every UK garden has had at some point. Cheap new, cheap used, easy to repair. Not glamorous, but it does the job for years if you change the oil.
mowers.tip: Used SP46s should be £100–160 — never pay more than £180. Massive supply on Marketplace. Look for visible engine oil leaks around the dipstick (a common fault). The plastic rear flap hinge cracks — check it pivots freely. If the drive cable feels loose, factor £15 for a replacement.

mowers.tagline: British-built premium roller — the Land Rover of mowers
pros: Beautiful stripes from the heavy rear roller
cons: Expensive new and only fair value used
41cm cut narrower than rivals at this price
Heavier deck means harder to manoeuvre
mowers.verdict: A connoisseur's mower. If you care about a perfect striped lawn and want a machine your grandchild could rebuild, the Harrier is the answer. Overkill for casual gardens.
mowers.tip: £380–500 is realistic for a tidy used Harrier 41. Hayter parts are still made in Britain so even old ones are repairable — that adds value. Test the variable speed lever travels through full range. Check the rear roller turns true and bearings are silent. Original Hayter spec sticker on the deck adds £40 to the price.

pros: Stihl build quality and dealer support
Engine is quiet for a petrol
cons: Slightly more expensive than equivalent Mountfield
Mono handle awkward for tall users
mowers.verdict: A solid choice if you have a Stihl dealer nearby. Good engineering, good dealer network, but you pay for the badge. Buy only if you value the support — otherwise the Mountfield does the same job for less.
mowers.tip: £300–400 used is fair. Always ask if it has been Stihl-serviced — yes adds £50 to fair price. The mono-handle pivots — check it does so cleanly without play. Stihl chassis numbers verify age via dealer; do not skip that step on anything over £350.

mowers.tagline: Briggs-powered budget self-propelled with rear roller
pros: Rear roller for stripes at budget price
cons: Plastic wheels and deck feel cheaper
Roller bearings degrade quicker than premium brands
mowers.verdict: The smart-money budget choice if you want stripes without paying Hayter or Honda money. Briggs engines are easy to service. Performs above its price.
mowers.tip: £140–220 is the used range — anything under £150 is excellent if it runs cleanly. Check the rear roller for play — a worn bearing is £20 to replace yourself. Briggs engines are easy to spot-service: change oil and air filter and most issues vanish. Avoid if rust on the deck looks structural rather than surface.

pros: Cheapest reputable petrol mower in the UK
Light enough to lift into a car
Push only — fewer things to break
Webb dealer network covers most of the UK
cons: Push only is hard work on slopes
Engine is no-name OEM, not Briggs or Honda
Resale value drops to nothing fast
mowers.verdict: A perfectly fine first petrol mower for a small flat lawn. Do not expect it to last 15 years like a Honda. Treat as disposable.
mowers.tip: Used Webb Classic should be £70–120. If asking £150+, walk — buy new from B&Q for £230 instead. Look for fuel staining around the carb (sign of stale petrol left over winter). The engine is generic Chinese-made, so no real brand value — only buy if it starts on the day of viewing.

pros: Same Stiga engine as the SP46 for less money
Push-only means fewer cables and parts to fail
Light enough for an average user
cons: Push-only is tiring on big lawns
mowers.verdict: The cheap-and-cheerful Mountfield for buyers who do not need self-propel. Same engine as the SP46, half the secondhand price. If you have a flat lawn and decent fitness, ignore the SP46 and buy this.
mowers.tip: £60–110 used is the right zone — these come up constantly on Marketplace. Same engine checks as the SP46: no oil leaks, golden oil, fires on first or second pull. The HP46 is the most plentiful petrol mower in the UK — never overpay; another will appear next week.

mowers.tagline: Cheap self-propelled petrol with steel deck
pros: 51cm cut is wide for the price
Steel deck unusual at this price point
cons: Hyundai branded but engine is generic
Parts can be slow to source
mowers.verdict: Punches above its weight on cut width and deck quality. The engine is the weak link — capable but not premium. Good value new, fair value used.
mowers.tip: £130–200 is realistic used. Steel deck means rust is the killer — flip it and inspect the underside thoroughly. Engine starts can degrade over winter; ask the seller to fire it from cold. The 51cm blade is unusual so spares cost more than 46cm rivals — factor that in.

mowers.tagline: Premium Stiga with twin-clip blade and rear roller
pros: Twin-clip blade gives finer cut and more box capacity
70L grass box rare at this price
cons: Twin-clip blade more expensive to replace
mowers.verdict: The thinking person's alternative to a Honda HRX. Twin-clip blade actually does what Stiga claims and the rear roller stripes well. Fair value new, good value used.
mowers.tip: £240–340 is fair used. Twin-clip blade is the differentiator — make sure it is intact and not bent (a £35 part). Rear roller bearings should turn smoothly. Stiga share parts with Mountfield so service is straightforward at any Stiga or Mountfield dealer.

mowers.tagline: Corded mower with rear roller for stripes
pros: Corded means no battery to fail
Rear roller for stripes — rare on electric
cons: Cable management on big lawns is annoying
mowers.verdict: The best corded mower you can buy and a quiet legend on UK lawns. If your lawn is small-to-medium and near a power point, this beats every cordless rival on cost-per-year. Stripes on a £200 mower is genuinely unusual.
mowers.tip: £60–100 is fair used — enormous supply. Check the cord for tape repairs (rewire £15 if needed). Roller should turn smoothly. The motor is brushless and rarely fails — most Rotaks die from cable damage, not motor wear. A cheap one with a dodgy cable is still a bargain.

mowers.tagline: Iconic budget corded with rear roller
cons: Motor windings can fail after heavy use
mowers.verdict: The mower in 2 million UK sheds. Not built to last 20 years but cheap enough that you do not need it to. Buys out the corded category on price.
mowers.tip: £25–55 used and you cannot really go wrong. Massive supply means walk away from anything dirty or with cable repairs — the next one will appear tomorrow. Listen for unusual whining from the motor (worn brushes); if heard, halve the price.

mowers.tagline: Budget corded for very small lawns
mowers.verdict: A good first mower for a tiny terraced garden. If your lawn is over 40m², look elsewhere. Disposable but fine for the price.
mowers.tip: £20–45 used. Honestly, just buy new for £89 unless you find one for £30. Check the cable and motor noise. No serviceable parts — it either runs or it does not.

mowers.tagline: Bigger corded Rotak for medium lawns
pros: Comb edges for cutting close to walls
mowers.verdict: The roller-less variant of the Rotak 36 R. Same Bosch DNA, slightly cheaper but you lose the stripes. If stripes are not a priority, it is the smarter buy.
mowers.tip: £55–90 used. Same checks as Rotak 36 R — cable condition, motor noise. The lack of rear roller actually means fewer parts to fail; in practice these last longer than the rollered version.

pros: EGO 56V batteries cross-compatible across full range
cons: Battery and charger eat the price
mowers.verdict: The cordless that finally killed the "cordless cannot replace petrol" argument. If your lawn is up to 1500m² and you can charge between sessions, this is the smartest mower on the market right now.
mowers.tip: £380–520 used. Always insist on the original EGO battery (count the cells through the charge ports). Aftermarket batteries break this mower. Test runtime — a healthy 5Ah does 35–45 minutes; anything under 25 minutes means battery is gone (£200 to replace). EGO retain value better than any other cordless brand.

cons: Can struggle in long wet grass
mowers.verdict: The "right size" EGO for typical UK gardens. If your lawn is under 1000m², this is the better buy than the flagship — same quality, lower price, same battery platform.
mowers.tip: £280–400 fair used. Same battery checks as the LM2135 — original EGO only. Look at the rear-flap hinge for cracks. Self-propel drive belt is the most common wear part — listen for slipping under load on the test drive.

pros: Stihl AP battery shared across pro tools
cons: Battery and charger sold separately — adds £300+
Limited runtime on a single AP300 battery
mowers.verdict: Buy this only if you already own Stihl AP-platform tools. Otherwise the all-in-the-box EGO offers more for the money. Beautifully built but the battery economics only work for existing Stihl owners.
mowers.tip: £380–550 with battery is fair used — without battery, halve that. AP battery cells degrade like any other; insist on a runtime demo. Stihl chassis numbers tie to dealer service history — get them checked on anything over £450.

mowers.tagline: Compact 36V cordless on PowerForAll Alliance battery
pros: Husqvarna build on a PowerForAll battery
Battery shared with Bosch home tools
mowers.verdict: A Husqvarna badge on a small mower aimed at urban gardens. Useful if you already own PowerForAll tools. Otherwise the Bosch CityMower 18 is the same idea for less.
mowers.tip: £120–200 used. Confirm the PowerForAll battery is included — without it, this mower is worthless. The motor is shared across Bosch and Husqvarna so spares are easy. New is £329; pay no more than £200 used.

pros: Includes battery and charger in price
40V Greenworks platform crosses to other tools
cons: Greenworks battery resale value is poor
mowers.verdict: A solid budget cordless if you want a complete kit and do not mind pushing. The 40V Greenworks platform is decent but locks you in. Fair value new, good value used.
mowers.tip: £90–160 used. Battery health is everything — demand a runtime test. A healthy 4Ah does 30 minutes; under 15 minutes means dead and replacement batteries are £80+. Avoid any with chewed cable insulation around the battery contacts (overheating sign).

cons: Greenworks 60V batteries less common than EGO/Stihl
mowers.verdict: The cheapest serious cordless with stripes. Performance is genuinely close to a Honda HRG. Battery resale is the weakness — if you want long-term value, EGO wins.
mowers.tip: £200–340 fair used. Roller bearings, drive cable, battery health — same checks as a petrol SP. Greenworks 60V batteries depreciate fast — never pay more than £100 for a used 6Ah pack.

cons: Twin-battery design halves runtime if one is older
mowers.verdict: The "I already own Ryobi" mower. If your shed has 5+ Ryobi 18V tools, this slots in beautifully. Otherwise the 18V platform is overworked here and a 40V/56V rival cuts better.
mowers.tip: £100–180 used — strongly check both batteries individually. Mismatched battery pairs (one new, one tired) give 10 minutes of runtime. Insist on a real-world cut test. Ryobi ONE+ batteries are the most counterfeited — buy from a seller who can show original receipts.

pros: Makita LXT batteries are the gold standard for resale
mowers.verdict: A trade-grade mower built like Makita's drills. Pricey to kit out but will outlast almost every rival. The LXT battery resale alone is worth the entry.
mowers.tip: £170–280 with batteries is fair used. Without batteries, ignore — bare DLM382 should be £80 max. LXT batteries hold value at £40–60 each used. The motor is bombproof — most issues are battery-related.

cons: Twin 18V drains fast on big lawns
mowers.verdict: A surprising performer — the 53cm cut and decent runtime make this a real petrol alternative for a Makita owner. Outsider buys should still pick EGO; the battery-platform lock-in matters.
mowers.tip: £250–400 with batteries is fair used. Self-propel cable is the wear point — check it adjusts smoothly. As with the DLM382, batteries are 90% of the value. A bare body-only DLM539Z is worth £150 max.

cons: Worx batteries do not hold value
mowers.verdict: The smart budget cordless. Genuinely usable for small lawns and the included batteries cross to Worx tools you may already own. Outclassed at £400+ but unbeatable value at £200 new.
mowers.tip: £60–130 used. Check both batteries individually as with any twin-battery design. The plastic deck cracks if used on uneven ground — flip it and look for hairlines around the blade mount. Worx batteries are heavily counterfeited — only buy from sellers with original receipts.

pros: Bosch PowerForAll battery shared across home tools
mowers.verdict: The right tool for a postage-stamp lawn. Do not buy if your lawn is bigger than 100m². Bosch makes them durable — a fair urban mower.
mowers.tip: £50–110 used. PowerForAll battery is £40+ secondhand, so a battery-included sale is the only one worth doing. Check the battery cells via runtime — a healthy 4Ah does 25 mins; under 12 mins is a dead pack. Bosch motor is brushless and rarely fails.

cons: Battery sold separately on some configs
mowers.verdict: A neat continental-style cordless that suits small UK lawns. Lacks mulch and roller so functionality is basic. Gardena hold value better than Worx, less than EGO.
mowers.tip: £100–180 used. Confirm PowerForAll 40V battery is included. The handle latches can break — check they lock cleanly. No mulch plug is a real limitation; budget for bagging every cut.

pros: AK battery shared with Stihl home tools
cons: Stihl AK battery 30 mins per charge max
mowers.verdict: The Stihl entry-point. Build quality is genuinely Stihl, but the AK platform is intentionally limited to small jobs. Buy only if you already own Stihl AK tools.
mowers.tip: £70–150 used. Confirm AK battery is included with the sale. Stihl AK batteries have a 5-pin lock that can wear — check the battery clips firmly into place. Stihl chassis number checks add £30 to fair value if dealer-verified.

mowers.tagline: Budget 40V Cobra with mulch and collect
cons: Cobra battery platform isolated — no cross-tool benefit
mowers.verdict: A reasonable budget cordless if you do not care about ecosystem lock-in. The Greenworks G40 does the same job slightly better and has a bigger user base.
mowers.tip: £90–170 used. Cobra batteries hold no resale value, so a tired battery destroys the deal. Always demand a 20-minute runtime demo. The deck is plastic and the wheel mounts crack — inspect them carefully.

mowers.tagline: Boundary-wire entry robot for compact gardens
mowers.verdict: The robot lawnmower that proved the category. Handles UK weather, easy parts supply, dealer-serviceable. The 305 is the entry — fine for under 600m² lawns with simple shapes.
mowers.tip: £300–550 used. Boundary wire integrity is everything — walk the perimeter and check for breaks (visible cuts in the wire). Confirm app account is transferable or factory-reset performed before purchase. Charging contacts on the dock corrode — clean them with a wire brush before judging battery life.
mowers.imgFile: Husqvarna Automower 308 with track marks in lawn.jpg

mowers.tagline: EPOS satellite-guided pro robot — no boundary wire
pros: EPOS satellite — no boundary wire to install or break
mowers.verdict: The robot lawnmower people who own estates buy. EPOS is genuinely transformative — no boundary wire, you draw zones in an app. Worth every penny if your lawn justifies it.
mowers.tip: £1100–1800 used is realistic. EPOS reference station MUST be included — without it, this robot is a paperweight. Confirm satellite lock at the property before buying. Husqvarna serial number checks confirm warranty transferability — do this on any used purchase over £1500.

mowers.tagline: AWD wire-free robot with 75% slope ability
pros: 40cm cut is huge for a robot
cons: Mammotion support in UK is limited
RTK base station setup is complex
mowers.verdict: The robot that has Husqvarna nervous. AWD on slopes is a genuine breakthrough and the cut width of 40cm shrinks mowing time dramatically. Buy with caution — Mammotion is new in the UK and parts/service are still scaling.
mowers.tip: £1700–2400 used. Confirm the RTK base station and antenna are both present. Demand a live cut demo before paying — these are still rare so verify it actually works on the user's lawn. Reset the Mammotion account before buyer takes ownership.

mowers.tagline: Wire-free RTK robot for compact lawns
pros: Wire-free RTK at a sub-£1500 price
cons: RTK signal can drop near tall buildings
mowers.verdict: A genuinely interesting alternative to the Husqvarna 305 if you do not want to lay boundary wire. RTK works well in open suburban gardens; less reliable near trees or fences.
mowers.tip: £800–1200 used. Most YUKA Mini units are still under warranty — confirm purchase date with the seller. RTK base station must be included. Test it cuts a full perimeter before paying — RTK setup can be fragile. Mammotion accounts must be reset by seller.

pros: Segway support is improving in UK
cons: 18cm cut is small for an 800m² lawn
mowers.verdict: Segway took longer than Husqvarna to get the basics right but the H800E is solid. Reliable and well-supported now. The 18cm cut is the only limitation — it is slow.
mowers.tip: £1000–1700 used. VisionFence base must be included with calibration intact. Test it in mixed lighting if possible — heavy rain can fool the cameras. Segway accounts transfer cleanly if seller does the factory reset; insist on this.

mowers.tagline: Compact RTK robot for small gardens
mowers.verdict: A reasonable urban-garden robot at the entry of the wire-free category. Husqvarna 305 still wins on durability and dealer support, but if no boundary wire is the priority, the i108E is the cheap way in.
mowers.tip: £400–700 used. RTK base must be included with mounting bracket. Most i108E units are recent — confirm purchase date and warranty status. Reset Segway account before purchase. Avoid units stored outside; the dock electronics corrode in damp UK weather.

pros: Worx PowerShare battery shared with other tools
mowers.verdict: The accessible robot. Will not last as long as a Husqvarna 305 but costs half as much used. A reasonable starter robot for a small simple lawn.
mowers.tip: £200–400 used. Boundary wire is the #1 fail point — walk every metre and look for breaks. Worx battery cells are the second fail point; demand a runtime test. Reset the Worx app account before purchase. Avoid any with rust on the underside — sign of being left out in winter.

mowers.tagline: Quiet boundary-wire robot for tiny lawns
mowers.verdict: The right robot for a small flat suburban lawn. Quiet, neat, reliable, and Gardena have been making robots for years. If your lawn is under 250m², it is the safer choice over the new Mammotion or Segway entry models.
mowers.tip: £170–340 used. Boundary wire integrity check as always. Gardena dock contacts are gold-plated and rarely corrode — that is a quality marker. Confirm the seller has the original cutting blades stored (they wear and 4-pack costs £15). Reset the Gardena Smart app before purchase.
mowers.imgNote: Gardena robotic mower (family match for Sileno Minimo)

cons: Big and heavy — needs storage space
Service costs higher than budget rivals
mowers.verdict: The default sensible ride-on for 0.5–1 acre UK lawns. JD pedigree, parts everywhere, holds value remarkably well. Spend slightly more, save it back on resale.
mowers.tip: £1700–2500 used. Engine hours matter more than years — under 200 hours is excellent, over 600 means assume the worst. Test the PTO clutch — should engage smoothly. Hydrostatic transmission should pull cleanly forward and back with no jolts. JD service history adds £300 to fair value.
mowers.imgFile: Hedge and ditch trimming with a John Deere X300R ride-on mower - geograph.org.uk - 7023613.jpg

mowers.verdict: For estate-size lawns and people who want the best mid-size ride-on you can buy. The 4-wheel steering really does help around obstacles. Pricey but earns it back in time saved.
mowers.tip: £2800–4200 used. Engine hours under 400 is excellent. 4WS linkage components wear — check tracking on a flat surface for pull. Insist on full JD service history. Anti-scalp wheels on the deck show heavy use; replacements are £30 each.

cons: Stiga dealer network thinner than JD
Resale value below JD or Honda
mowers.verdict: The JD X167R's direct rival at a lower price. Cuts as well, holds value less. Buy if budget is tight, JD if you can stretch.
mowers.tip: £900–1500 used. Stiga engine hours easier to ignore than JD because spare parts cost less — but still ask. Hydrostatic drive belt is the wear point — slipping under load means £80 belt and dealer fitting. Anti-scalp wheels and deck wash port are check items.

cons: Cub Cadet UK dealer support thin
mowers.verdict: A solid heavy-duty alternative to JD that punches above its price. The Kohler engine is a real plus. Just be aware UK service is patchy.
mowers.tip: £1300–2200 used. Kohler engine hours under 400 is good. Cub Cadet steering box wears — check for play. PTO clutch must engage cleanly without slip. Service history is harder to verify (smaller dealer network) so weight that into the price.

mowers.tagline: Honda hydrostatic ride-on with side discharge
pros: Honda V-twin — the most reliable engine in the category
mowers.verdict: For people who care more about engine longevity than badge prestige. The Honda V-twin will run for 1500 hours with sensible service. Choose this over a JD if your priority is "still works in 15 years".
mowers.tip: £1700–2900 used. Honda engine hours under 500 is excellent — these run forever. Test the hydrostatic forward/reverse smoothness. Look at the deck spindles — bearing seizure is the most expensive ride-on fault and these are non-trivial to replace. Honda service stamps add £400 to fair value.

mowers.tagline: Compact hydro ride-on for sub-3000m² lawns
pros: Cheapest credible hydro ride-on in the UK
Compact enough to fit a small shed
mowers.verdict: The default first ride-on. Half the price of a JD, does the same job on most UK gardens, parts are easy. The pragmatic choice — not the prestige one.
mowers.tip: £500–1100 used. Engine hours under 300 is excellent. Hydrostatic drive belt slip under load is the most common fault — test on an incline. Steering linkage develops play; check it. Mountfield/Stiga share parts so service at any dealer is straightforward.

pros: PGC powered grass collector — handles wet grass
mowers.verdict: For estate owners, professional gardeners, and people with serious slopes. Countax build them like tractors and they last accordingly. The 4WD genuinely changes what is possible on a hilly lawn.
mowers.tip: £2200–4000 used. Honda engine hours under 600 is good. 4WD diff oil should be checked and the seller should know when it was last changed. PGC blower belt is the wear part — slipping means poor collection. Countax service history adds significantly to value as parts come from one source.

mowers.tagline: Compact British ride-on with rear collect
pros: 76cm deck small enough for narrow gates
Rear collect big enough for most jobs
cons: Briggs Intek not as durable as Honda V-twin
mowers.verdict: The right ride-on for a medium suburban lawn that will not fit anything bigger. Hayter quality means it lasts. Outclassed on price by Mountfield 1530H but the build is noticeably better.
mowers.tip: £900–1700 used. Engine hours under 400 is good. Hayter parts are still made in Britain so old machines remain serviceable — that supports value. Test the gear-change linkage; it should be precise. Rear collection chute kinks are a common fault — flex it during inspection.

pros: Cuts cleanly on short fine grass
cons: Useless on long or wet grass
mowers.verdict: A purist's mower for ornamental lawns. If you have a small flat lawn and like the meditative job, this is genuinely satisfying. Useless if you let the grass go more than a week.
mowers.tip: £15–40 used. Look for blade nicks — re-sharpening is £20 at any local mower shop. Roller bearings should turn freely. Avoid any with bent handles; cylinder mower handles are brand-specific and hard to replace. Massive supply means there is no need to overpay.

mowers.tagline: Bosch hand-push cylinder with grass box
mowers.verdict: Compact and well-engineered for very small ornamental lawns. The grass box is the differentiator — most manual cylinders mulch only. Disappointingly small cut though.
mowers.tip: £20–45 used. Bosch builds to last so even old units are fine. Check the cylinder blades for nicks (re-sharpening £20). Grass box hinges are plastic and crack — inspect them. Bosch parts are rare but rarely needed.

pros: Cheapest manual cylinder with grass box
cons: Cylinder blades are basic steel — dull faster
mowers.verdict: A perfectly fine first manual cylinder for a tiny lawn. Will not match the Webb or Bosch on long-term durability but the price is right.
mowers.tip: £15–35 used. As with all manual cylinders, sharpness is everything — test cut on a tuft of grass before paying. Re-sharpening is £20 if needed. Plastic wheels can crack if stored cold and dropped — inspect closely.

mowers.tagline: The most common used Honda on UK Marketplace
mowers.verdict: The IZY badge is what most UK buyers actually mean when they say "I want a Honda for the lawn." Cheap to buy, easy to maintain, and the engine outlasts everything around it. The smartest budget Honda on the used market.
mowers.tip: £180–280 used is the sweet spot. Avoid anything over £320 — there is always another. Pull the dipstick: golden good, black means no service history. Plastic deck cracks around the wheel mounts after 8+ years — flip it and inspect. Genuine Honda blade is £20 if missing.

mowers.tagline: SP46 predecessor — UK best-seller in the 2010s
pros: Same Stiga engine family as the SP46
Light enough to lift into a car
mowers.verdict: The mower that paved the way for the SP46. Older but still capable and the used supply is huge — typically £20–40 cheaper than an SP46 in equivalent condition. If you find a clean one, snap it up.
mowers.tip: £70–140 is the realistic used range. Massive Marketplace volume — never overpay. Test self-propel under load. Drive cable replacement is £15 DIY. Avoid any with melted plastic around the muffler — sign of dry-running and overheating.

mowers.tagline: British roller petrol below the Harrier price point
pros: Cheaper entry to the Hayter lineup
mowers.verdict: The "I want a Hayter but the Harrier is too much" buy. Same British dealer support, same parts longevity, same striping pedigree. Push-only is the trade-off — fine for under 800m² lawns.
mowers.tip: £200–340 is fair used. Briggs engines are plentiful in spares. Check the rear roller for stripe alignment — uneven roller wear is the #1 fault. Hayter parts are still British-made; that holds value over Mountfield/Stiga rivals.

pros: Self-propelled with proper lawn tractor feel
cons: Steel deck rusts if stored damp
New price ambitious vs Hayter Spirit
mowers.verdict: The mower that defined "the British striped lawn" before everyone went Honda. Atco is now part of STIGA Group but the Liner still runs the original heavy-roller deck. A connoisseur classic on Marketplace.
mowers.tip: £200–400 used and the spread is wide because condition varies massively. Steel deck rust is the make-or-break — flip it and look for hole-through corrosion (not just surface). Original Atco serial sticker on the deck adds £40. Avoid anything stored outside year-round.

mowers.tagline: Iconic UK budget electric — Argos legacy
pros: Cheapest reputable electric with rear roller
Rear roller for stripes — rare at this price
cons: Motor windings can fail after 5+ years
mowers.verdict: The mower in millions of UK sheds. Qualcast/Suffolk/Argos lineage means people inherit them, sell them, and buy another. Disposable but legendary value at sub-£30 used.
mowers.tip: £15–35 used and Marketplace has dozens. Cable repair is the most common fault — £10 to rewire if you can. Listen for motor whine (worn brushes). Rear roller bearings should turn freely. If the seller will not run it on the day, walk away.

mowers.tagline: Iconic British petrol cylinder — striping legend
pros: Properly heavy roller — best stripes of any mower
Cylinder cut is finer than any rotary
cons: Useless on long or wet grass
mowers.verdict: Not made any more, but the Suffolk Punch is the mower bowling-green keepers and ornamental-lawn obsessives still hunt for. If you have a flat formal lawn, nothing beats the cut and the stripes. Marketplace is the only way to buy one.
mowers.tip: £150–400 used depending on condition and age. Cylinder sharpness is the entire game — test cut on a piece of paper at the deck (it should slice cleanly). Re-grind is £40–60. Avoid welded frame repairs. Original Qualcast/Suffolk decals add £30 for collectors.

pros: Hovers — no wheels means awkward shapes are easy
mowers.verdict: The mower that defined the hover category and still the easiest answer for sloped, awkward, or banked lawns. Not for big jobs but unbeatable on the small stuff that wheeled mowers struggle with.
mowers.tip: £15–35 used. Cable damage is the #1 fault — check end-to-end. The lift fan housing develops cracks; flip it and inspect. Bag clip plastic snaps — replacement bag is £15 if missing. Massive Argos/B&Q used supply.

pros: Bigger 33cm cut than the Hover Vac
Mulching design — no box to empty
Glides over banks and slopes effortlessly
cons: Cable on hilly lawns is fiddly
mowers.verdict: The right tool for sloped or oddly-shaped lawns. Mulching means you do not stop to empty a box. Wheeled mowers cannot match a hover for getting under fences and over banks.
mowers.tip: £25–55 used. Same checks as the Hover Vac — cable, fan housing, blade condition. The mulching plate at the deck base wears; replacement is £15. Glider 330s last 10+ years if stored indoors.

pros: B&S engine — well known and serviceable
B&Q dealer support across the UK
cons: 3-year B&Q warranty is generous but limited to original buyer
mowers.verdict: The default "I just need a petrol mower from B&Q" buy. Performs above its price thanks to the Briggs engine, but build quality is honestly below Mountfield. Decent value new, fair used.
mowers.tip: £70–160 used. Briggs engine is the only durable bit — if it runs cleanly cold, the rest can be fixed. Drive cable and plastic chassis cracks are the common faults. Never pay more than £150 — there is always another at B&Q for £270 new with warranty.

mowers.tagline: Predecessor of the modern Rotak — huge used supply
pros: Bosch reliability at sub-£40 used prices
cons: Cable on bigger lawns gets in the way
mowers.verdict: The little Bosch that quietly outlasted everything around it. A genuine bargain on Marketplace — most still run after 10–15 years. The Rotak 32 is the "first mower" answer for tiny urban lawns.
mowers.tip: £20–45 used. Cable condition is everything. Bosch motors are brushless and rarely fail — if it runs and the cable is intact, it will keep running. The blade hub is plastic; replacement is £8. Genuine Bosch blade £12.

pros: Cheap used and easy to find
cons: Motor windings can fail after heavy use
Cable management issues on bigger lawns
mowers.verdict: The corded mower most British DIYers buy at Argos when their old one dies. Performs the job well enough; will not last 20 years like a Bosch. Pure utility purchase.
mowers.tip: £25–55 used. Cable damage and motor whine are the two killers — listen carefully. Plastic chassis around the handle joint cracks; check it pivots cleanly. Replacement blades are £10 from any DIY shop.

pros: Parkside 20V battery shared across cordless tools
cons: Battery sold separately on some configs
Parkside support is via Lidl returns only
mowers.verdict: The Lidl Middle Aisle special that has built a genuine cult following. If you already own Parkside 20V tools, this slots into the ecosystem at a price that no other brand can match. Surprisingly capable for the money.
mowers.tip: £25–60 used. Insist the 20V battery is included — without it, this mower is useless. Battery cells degrade like any other; demand a runtime test (15 minutes minimum on a healthy 4Ah). Parkside warranty is non-transferable so used buyers are on their own.

cons: Ferrex battery isolated — no cross-tool benefit
mowers.verdict: Aldi’s answer to Lidl Parkside. The 40V platform genuinely outperforms most sub-£200 cordless rivals when the Specialbuy lands. Catch it in stock and it is a steal.
mowers.tip: £35–80 used. Ferrex batteries are unbranded clones — verify the cells deliver real runtime before paying. Replacement batteries are scarce after 18 months because Aldi rotates suppliers. Treat as semi-disposable.

mowers.tagline: Argos mid-tier corded with rear roller
pros: Rear roller for stripes at sub-£150
mowers.verdict: A genuine bargain on the Argos shelf. Stripes from a £150 corded mower is rare — it is the obvious step up from a Flymo or Bosch budget unit. Build quality is the trade-off.
mowers.tip: £30–80 used. Cable and motor noise are the two checks. Roller bearings should turn freely. The handle latches are plastic — they break and replacements are not stocked, so check before buying.

mowers.verdict: Stihl arrived late to robotics but the iMOW 6 is genuinely competitive with the Husqvarna 410 NERA. Worth considering if you already trust Stihl petrol kit and have a Stihl dealer nearby.
mowers.tip: £1300–1900 used (rare on Marketplace yet — most are still under warranty). RTK reference station must be included. Verify dealer warranty transfer — Stihl handles this cleanly via chassis number. Reset the iMOW Cloud account before purchase.

pros: Right-sized for typical UK suburban lawns
cons: 22cm cut means slow on biggest lawns
mowers.verdict: The Husqvarna sweet-spot for 2024. Smaller and cheaper than the 430X NERA, EPOS-enabled (unlike the 305), and right for 1000–1500m² gardens. Likely to be the best-selling robot of the year.
mowers.tip: £900–1500 used (still rare). EPOS reference station is mandatory and must be included. Confirm satellite lock at the buyer’s property before payment. Husqvarna serial-number warranty checks confirm transferability.

pros: XP series outperforms most petrol rivals
Same 56V platform across all EGO tools
mowers.verdict: EGO put the petrol manufacturers on notice with the XP line. The LMX5300SP genuinely matches a Honda HRX on cut quality and out-runs most cordless rivals on runtime. The new flagship.
mowers.tip: £550–800 used. Demand the XP battery (not standard 56V — check the cell count and the XP label). Aftermarket batteries do not work on this generation. EGO retain value — never pay under £450 for a clean unit; equally never over £800.

pros: 30cm cut sized between YUKA Mini and LUBA 2 5000
mowers.verdict: The right Mammotion if your lawn is too big for the YUKA Mini but too small to justify the 5000. AWD opens up sloped lawns that nothing else handles. Buy with caution given Mammotion is still new in the UK.
mowers.tip: £1000–1600 used (rare yet — most under warranty). RTK base and antenna both required. Test on the buyer’s actual lawn before paying. Reset the Mammotion app account before transfer.

mowers.tagline: Boundary-wire Bosch robot for medium lawns
pros: Bosch app integration with Smart Home
cons: Indego app updates have broken older units
mowers.verdict: The Bosch alternative to a Husqvarna 305 for buyers who prefer the Bosch Smart Home ecosystem. LogiCut is genuinely smarter than random-walk robots. Boundary wire is the limit.
mowers.tip: £250–500 used. Boundary wire integrity is everything — walk every metre. Bosch dock contacts last well, but check the rubber bumper for cracks. Reset the Indego app before purchase. Avoid units stored outdoors year-round.

pros: Camera-based — no boundary wire and no RTK base needed
mowers.verdict: Worx made the boldest move in robotics with the Vision line — no wire, no RTK base, just a camera. It works well in good light and is genuinely cheaper than the wire-free competition. Watch this category.
mowers.tip: £500–900 used. Confirm firmware is up to date — early Vision units shipped with bugs that later firmware fixed. Camera lens scratches are a problem; inspect closely. Reset the Worx Landroid app before purchase.

pros: Honda dealer support across the UK
mowers.verdict: A premium boundary-wire robot from a brand that knows engines but is newer to robotics. The Miimo line has caught up to Husqvarna on durability — pricing is the only concern.
mowers.tip: £900–1500 used. Honda Miimo serial-number checks confirm warranty transferability — do this on anything over £1100. Boundary wire integrity check as standard. Reset Miimo Live account before purchase.
mowers.imgNote: Miimo 310 — same Miimo family as HRM 70 Live

mowers.verdict: The Ryobi mower that finally competes with EGO and Greenworks 60V at the wide-cut end. If you already own Ryobi 36V tools, the platform fit is the real value.
mowers.tip: £200–340 used. Confirm 36V battery is genuine Ryobi MAX (counterfeits are common). 5Ah battery healthy runtime is 35–45 minutes. Self-propel drive belt is the wear part — listen for slip under load.

cons: Stiga UK robot dealer network thinner than Husqvarna
mowers.verdict: Stiga’s answer to the Husqvarna 410 NERA at a lower price. Build quality is solid (it shares engineering with Mountfield). Worth checking if you have a Stiga or Mountfield dealer locally.
mowers.tip: £600–1000 used. RTK base required. Confirm satellite lock at the property. Stiga dealer network is patchy on robotics — verify local support before buying. Reset Stiga Smart account before purchase.

mowers.tagline: British-built ride-on with PGC — Countax twin
pros: PGC powered grass collector — handles wet
mowers.verdict: Identical engineering to the Countax B65 from the same AriensCo UK factory. Westwood badging traditionally appears at slightly lower prices. A genuine British-built tractor for serious lawns.
mowers.tip: £1800–3500 used. Engine hours under 600 is good. PGC blower belt slipping is the most common collection fault — £40 replacement, dealer fitting recommended. Westwood/Countax service history is interchangeable; both add £300 to fair value.

cons: Battery cells degrade faster than Husqvarna
mowers.verdict: The accessible robot for buyers who want to try the category without spending £1000+. Will not match a Husqvarna 305 for longevity but costs half as much used. A reasonable starter for a small simple lawn.
mowers.tip: £100–250 used. Boundary wire is the #1 fail point — walk every metre. Yard Force batteries lose capacity after 2–3 years; demand a runtime test. Reset the Yard Force app before purchase. Avoid any with rust on the underside.

mowers.tagline: Premium 53cm Honda — bigger sibling to the HRX 476
pros: Honda GCV200 — bigger engine, same legendary reliability
cons: Wide deck means narrow gates may be a problem
mowers.verdict: For lawns over 1500m² where the HRX 476 starts feeling small. Same engine pedigree, 6cm more cut, twice the bag — finishes a half-acre lawn in one bag-empty rather than three. The Honda owners who upgrade rarely go back.
mowers.tip: £600–800 used is the right zone. Engine hours under 200 means a clean buy. Test the variable-speed lever through full range — sticky lever is a £40 cable swap. The wider deck makes the rear roller bearings work harder; check for play before paying.

mowers.tagline: Electric-start Honda IZY for medium UK lawns
pros: Electric start removes the pull-cord issue
cons: Battery for the start motor adds a wear part
Heavier than the rope-start HRG 466 SK
mowers.verdict: The right Honda for buyers who never want to pull a rope. The electric start is what separates this from the rope-start HRG 466 SK — at the cost of an extra £50 used and one more thing to fail. Worth it for older or less able operators.
mowers.tip: £320–420 used is realistic. Test the electric start cold — a slow turn-over means the battery is dying (£35 replacement). Otherwise same checks as any IZY: golden oil, clean rope-start as fallback, drive cable not slipping under load.

mowers.tagline: 53cm Honda IZY — the wider IZY for bigger lawns
pros: Honda GCV170 — bigger engine for the wider deck
53cm cut beats the 466 by a useful margin
Same IZY simplicity (rope start, single drive lever)
cons: Wider deck means gate clearance issues for some
mowers.verdict: For people who liked the IZY 466 but want the cut width of an HRX without the price. Same simple format, just bigger. If your lawn is between 1000–1500m² and stripes don't matter, this is the smarter Honda buy than stretching to a 537.
mowers.tip: £420–540 used is fair. Drive belt under load is the wear point — slipping when self-propel is engaged means a £30 replacement. Check the wider deck underside for impact damage; the 53cm cut is more likely to clip stones in narrow lawns.

pros: Cheapest petrol mower with a brand name
Push-only means fewer parts to fail
cons: 41cm cut narrow for typical UK gardens
100cc engine works hard on long grass
mowers.verdict: The mower in the corner of B&Q for £159. Not glamorous, not durable, but cheaper than half the cordless options for similar capability. Treat as semi-disposable — five seasons is realistic.
mowers.tip: £40–80 used. Massive supply, never overpay. Pull-start should fire on first pull when warm. Plastic deck cracks around the wheel mounts after 6+ years — flip and inspect. A clean unit at £50 is a steal for an emergency replacement.

pros: Steel deck unusual at this price — outlasts plastic
cons: Push-only on a 28kg machine is hard work on slopes
Steel deck rusts if stored damp
mowers.verdict: The smart Mountfield for buyers who want longevity over self-propel. The steel deck is the killer feature — a plastic-decked SP46 will warp after a few years; this S42PD will still be cutting in 20. Buy if you have a flat lawn and a dry shed.
mowers.tip: £70–130 used. Steel deck rust is the make-or-break — flip and look for hole-through corrosion (not just surface rust). Engine checks same as any Stiga: dipstick golden, fires cold. Anything stored under cover is a buy; anything left outside, walk away.

mowers.tagline: Premium SP46 with Honda engine swap
pros: Honda GCV engine on a Mountfield body — best of both
Significantly more reliable than the standard SP46
Same parts/dealer support as the regular SP46
cons: £100 more new than the standard SP46
Used supply thinner than the rope-start variant
mowers.verdict: The Honda-engined SP46 is the bargain that nobody talks about. You get Honda longevity on a chassis with massive parts supply — and Mountfield resale economics. A used Honda HRX is £550. A used SP46H is £220. Same engine reliability for less than half the price.
mowers.tip: £170–280 used. Always confirm the Honda decals are genuine (Mountfield also sold a Briggs version called SP46B — different beast). Check the GCV engine number on the side cover. Honda-engined Mountfields are reliable and underpriced — an absolute value buy.

pros: 48cm cut + 70L box — generous for the price
Stiga ST160 engine handles long grass
cons: V-Drive cable is a known wear part
Stiga ST160 not as durable as a Honda GCV
mowers.verdict: Mountfield's answer to the Honda HRX 476 at half the price. Specs look identical on paper — variable-speed SP, rear roller, 48cm cut. The engine is the gap. If you cycle mowers every 7 years, this works out cheaper than a Honda. Beyond 10 years, the Honda wins.
mowers.tip: £200–340 used. V-Drive cable slipping under load is the #1 fault — test it on an incline. Roller bearings should turn freely. Honda owners shopping used would walk past this; Mountfield converts will love it. £220 with full service history is the sweet spot.

mowers.verdict: The cheapest Hayter you can buy. Build quality genuinely above Mountfield/Cobra at the same price — Hayter still makes them in Spalding. The Briggs engine is the trade-off: less smooth than Honda but parts are everywhere. A serious mower for someone with a small flat lawn.
mowers.tip: £140–230 used. Hayter parts are still made in Britain so even older units are repairable — that supports value. Briggs engine should fire on first pull when warm. Check the deck weld points; Hayter's pressed-steel decks last decades but show fatigue at the wheel mounts.

pros: 56cm British roller — the widest premium cut available
cons: Wide deck means narrow gates impossible
Premium price stretches even Hayter loyalists
mowers.verdict: The mower people who care about their lawn buy when they can afford to buy once. Hayter Harrier with a Honda engine is genuinely a forever machine — 30-year-old Harriers still come up serviced. The 56AD is the largest, most pro-grade Harrier; overkill for under 1500m² but transcendent for over.
mowers.tip: £600–900 used. Honda engine hours matter — under 200 is excellent. Hayter rear roller and AD drive linkage are the wear points; both serviceable at any Hayter dealer. Original Hayter spec sticker on the deck adds £80 to fair value. Anything with welded chassis repairs, walk away.

pros: Heavy steel roller for perfect stripes
cons: Pro-grade build is overkill for 95% of UK gardens
mowers.verdict: For groundsmen, cricket clubs, and people with formal ornamental lawns. The GXV engine is the industrial Honda used in pro stationary equipment — different beast from the GCV. Built to run 8 hours a day for 20 years. Unjustifiable for a domestic lawn but unbeatable for what it is.
mowers.tip: £800–1300 used (rare on Marketplace — these are usually traded between professionals). Engine hours under 1000 is excellent. Hayter Pro service stamps add £200. Avoid any with welded deck repairs or non-standard wheel sizes (sign of pro abuse).

pros: Stihl build quality at entry price
cons: Entry Stihl engine not as durable as the RM 448 series
mowers.verdict: A solid entry into the Stihl range if you have a flat lawn and a dealer nearby. Otherwise the Mountfield HP46 does the same job for half the price. Buy this only if you value the Stihl support more than the £150 saving.
mowers.tip: £150–230 used. Stihl chassis numbers verify dealer service history — get them checked on anything over £200. Entry Stihl engines work harder than the 448 series — listen for unusual vibration or smoke at full throttle.

mowers.tagline: Rear-discharge Stihl with mulch kit — pro lawn care
mowers.verdict: A landscaper's tool, not a domestic mower. The Kawasaki FJ180V is the engine in serious commercial kit — bomb-proof but loud. Discharge-only suits big rough lawns where collection is impractical. Buy this only if you're mowing acres or doing it for money.
mowers.tip: £800–1300 used. Kawasaki engine hours under 800 is good (these run for 2000+). Self-propel cable wear is the common fault. Look for a discharge chute that pivots cleanly — bent chutes are common from contractor abuse.

pros: Stihl AK battery shared with home tools (hedge trimmer, blower, strimmer)
Lighter and quieter than the AP-system mowers
Stihl quality for an urban lawn
cons: Limited runtime on a single AK 30 battery
mowers.verdict: The right Stihl for buyers who already own the AK home-and-garden range. The battery slots into all your other Stihl tools — that's the actual reason to buy. Otherwise the EGO 47cm at the same price obliterates this on cut width and runtime.
mowers.tip: £100–180 used (always confirm battery is included). Stihl AK batteries have a 5-pin lock — check the lock clip is firm. AK batteries lose 25% capacity by year 4; demand a runtime test. Stihl chassis number checks confirm warranty status.

cons: Motor windings can fail after heavy use
mowers.verdict: A reasonable mid-range corded electric from a brand that actually has UK dealer support. Outclassed by Bosch on motor longevity but matched on cut quality. The 5-year warranty is worth £20 of the price.
mowers.tip: £25–60 used. Cable damage and motor whine are the two checks. Cobra warranty is non-transferable so used buyers are on their own. Plastic deck cracks at the rear flap hinge — common fault, £15 part if you can find it.

mowers.tagline: 53cm Cobra petrol with rear roller — premium walk-behind
pros: 53cm cut at sub-£600 with rear roller
Briggs 750EX is a known reliable engine
Cobra UK dealer network for parts
Stripes from a budget brand — rare combination
cons: Cobra brand depreciates faster than Stihl/Honda
Roller bearings degrade quicker than premium
mowers.verdict: The smart-money premium Cobra. Rear roller stripes at this price typically means Mountfield SP485 territory — but Cobra's build is comparable and the Briggs 750EX is a trusted engine. Buy if you want stripes without paying £1000.
mowers.tip: £170–340 used. Briggs 750EX engine hours under 100 is excellent. Roller bearings are the wear point — check for play. Cobra's rear flap hinge is plastic and cracks; inspect closely. Anything with welded deck repairs, walk away.

mowers.tagline: 40V Cobra cordless with self-propel and roller
pros: Self-propelled cordless with rear roller — uncommon combination
Battery and charger included in price
cons: Self-propel drive belt wear is common
mowers.verdict: Cobra's answer to the EGO LM1900E for buyers who want self-propel + stripes from a battery mower. The kit is fine; the brand is the question. Cobra batteries lose half their resale by year 3, so don't expect to recoup much used.
mowers.tip: £140–240 used. Always insist on a runtime test — Cobra battery cells degrade faster than EGO/Makita. Self-propel belt slip under load is common; budget for a £25 belt swap if needed. Roller bearings same story as the petrol Cobras — check for play.

pros: Webb is a recognised UK brand
cons: Webb 36V battery platform is isolated — no cross-tool benefit
mowers.verdict: A perfectly fine first cordless for a small lawn at a genuine bargain price. Webb is a long-established UK brand even if today's products are mostly imported. The 36V battery is a sticking point — useless for anything else, no resale value.
mowers.tip: £40–90 used. Battery is the entire game — demand a 20-minute runtime test. Webb 36V batteries cost £60 to replace (which is more than half the used mower price). Plastic chassis cracks at the wheel mount; flip and inspect.

pros: Cheapest reputable petrol push mower in the UK
Light enough to lift into a car
Push only — fewer things to break
Webb dealer network covers most of UK
cons: Engine is no-name OEM, not Briggs or Honda
mowers.verdict: A perfectly fine first petrol mower for a small flat lawn. Do not expect it to last 15 years like a Honda. Treat as disposable.
mowers.tip: Used Webb WER41P should be £70–120. If asking £150+, walk — buy new from B&Q for £230 instead. Look for fuel staining around the carb (sign of stale petrol left over winter). Generic OEM engine has no real brand value — only buy if it starts on the day of viewing.

mowers.tagline: 43cm Hyundai self-propelled petrol with steel deck
pros: Steel deck unusual at this price
Cheap entry petrol from a recognised brand
cons: Parts can be slow to source
mowers.verdict: Punches above its weight on cut width and deck quality. The engine is the weak link — capable but not premium. Good value new, fair value used. The smarter buy than a Webb at £30 more.
mowers.tip: £90–150 used. Steel deck means rust is the killer — flip it and inspect the underside thoroughly. Engine starts can degrade over winter; ask the seller to fire it from cold. Generic engines have no real brand value but Hyundai parts are available.

mowers.tagline: 53cm Hyundai with electric start — wide budget petrol
cons: Electric start battery is a wear part
mowers.verdict: A genuine surprise on the spec sheet — electric start, rear roller, 53cm cut, all for under £400 new. The brand is the question; performance is fine. Used market hasn't caught up so resale is poor, but new buyers get a lot for the money.
mowers.tip: £140–260 used. Electric start battery slow to crank = dying battery (£35 swap). Roller bearings on a budget brand wear faster — check for play. Steel deck rust check under the cutting deck is critical. Generic engine but parts cheap and easy.

cons: Cable on bigger lawns is a pain
mowers.verdict: A perfectly fine first mower for a tiny urban garden. Outclassed by the Bosch Rotak 32 at £20 more, but unbeatable at sub-£80 if budget is tight. Treat as disposable — five years is realistic.
mowers.tip: £15–35 used. Massive supply on Marketplace. Cable damage is the killer — check end-to-end. Generic 1000W motor will run if it fires up. No serviceable parts — it either runs or it doesn't.

pros: Stiga ST160 engine handles long grass
Stiga Combi system — collect, mulch, side-discharge
cons: Stiga dealer network thinner than Mountfield
mowers.verdict: The Stiga answer to the Honda HRX 537. Same cut width, similar features, much lower used price. The engine is the gap — the ST160 is reliable but not Honda-level. For a 1500–2000m² lawn this is a smart Mountfield-family alternative.
mowers.tip: £260–400 used. Twin-clip blade should be intact (no bent edges). Stiga share parts with Mountfield so service at any Mountfield dealer is straightforward. Roller bearings should turn smoothly. Anything with engine hours over 200, demand a service.

pros: Mulching-only design = no bag emptying ever
cons: No collection — useless for first cut of spring
mowers.verdict: A specialist tool for buyers who never bag clippings. If you mulch religiously and accept that mulching needs short, dry grass, this is dramatically faster than a collect-and-empty rotary. For most British lawns, the bag is more useful than the mulch-only convenience.
mowers.tip: £120–240 used. Specialist mowers have a smaller pool of buyers — bargains appear. Stiga engine same checks. Mulch deck design means more grass build-up — flip and clean before judging. Anyone bagging on it has bypassed the mulch design and may have damaged the underdeck.

pros: Bosch reliability at the entry price point
Comb edges cut close to walls
mowers.verdict: The middle Rotak. Bigger than the 32, smaller than the 36 R. The right Bosch corded if your lawn is between 200-400m² and stripes don't matter. Same cable-and-motor checks as any Rotak.
mowers.tip: £25–55 used. Cable end-to-end check is the only critical inspection. Bosch motors are brushless and almost never fail; if it runs and the cable is intact, it will keep running for years. Listen for unusual whining (worn brushes); halve the price if you hear it.

pros: Bosch 36V PowerForAll battery shared with other tools
cons: Push only at this price point
Single battery limits runtime to ~30 minutes
mowers.verdict: The smart Bosch cordless if you already own PowerForAll 36V tools (drill, saw, blower). Otherwise the EGO LM1900E offers more cut width and better runtime at the same price. The cross-tool battery is the real win here.
mowers.tip: £140–230 used. PowerForAll battery is shared with Bosch home tools — confirm it's included. Battery cells degrade after 4 years; demand a runtime test. Mowers stored outdoors in winter usually have damaged battery contacts — inspect closely.

mowers.verdict: The Bosch entry cordless. Same chassis family as the AdvancedRotak with a smaller battery. If your lawn is under 300m², this saves £100 over the Advanced and the runtime is fine. Bigger and the battery becomes the bottleneck.
mowers.tip: £90–160 used. PowerForAll 2.5Ah battery is the entire game — counterfeits and worn cells are common. Demand a 15-minute runtime demo. Bosch motor longevity means the mower outlasts the battery — budget £80 for a 4Ah upgrade after year 4.

mowers.verdict: The Flymo hover for buyers who outgrow the Hover Vac. Bigger cut, better build, twin-handle for sloped lawns. Mulch-only is the design choice — accept it or buy a Hover Vac.
mowers.tip: £35–80 used. Lift fan housing crack is the killer — flip and inspect. Twin-handle pivot points should lock cleanly. Cable in good condition is non-negotiable. Plastic deck repairs are usually visible — walk away from anything welded.

pros: Cheapest reputable robot in the UK
cons: 17cm cut means slow on full perimeter
mowers.verdict: The accessible robot. Half the price of a Husqvarna 305 used, smaller coverage, simpler. Will not last 15 years like a Husqvarna but costs the right amount. Reasonable starter robot for a small flat lawn.
mowers.tip: £100–200 used. Boundary wire integrity is everything — walk every metre. Battery cells are the second fail point; demand a runtime test. Reset the Flymo app account before purchase. Avoid units stored outdoors year-round (dock contacts corrode).

mowers.verdict: Husqvarna Group's entry into the wire-free robot category at the Flymo price point. The Worx Landroid Vision is the closer rival; this is functionally similar but cheaper. Watch this category — vision-based robots are improving fast.
mowers.tip: £250–450 used. Camera lens scratches are critical — inspect closely. Confirm firmware is up to date (early units shipped with bugs). Reset the Flymo Smart app before purchase. Avoid any with rain damage to the camera housing.

pros: Power X-Change 18V batteries shared across Einhell range
Self-propelled with rear roller — uncommon at this price
cons: Twin-battery design halves runtime if one is older
mowers.verdict: The Einhell flagship cordless. Self-propelled, rear-roller, 47cm cut at £600 with batteries. The Power X-Change ecosystem is the actual value — if you own Einhell tools, the batteries cross over cleanly. Otherwise the EGO XP series is the better cordless for the money.
mowers.tip: £200–340 used. Test BOTH 18V batteries individually — mismatched packs (one new, one tired) give 10 minutes of runtime. PXC batteries hold value at £40 each used. Self-propel drive belt is the wear part — check it doesn't slip on an incline.

mowers.verdict: A good first mower for a postage-stamp lawn. If your lawn is over 50m², look elsewhere. Disposable but fine for the price.
mowers.tip: £10–30 used. Honestly, just buy new for £69 unless you find one for £20. Check the cable and motor noise. No serviceable parts — it either runs or it does not.

mowers.verdict: The right EGO for buyers who don't need self-propel. Same cut quality as the LM1900E with simpler mechanics. If your lawn is flat and under 800m², this saves £100 and one wear part (the drive cable).
mowers.tip: £200–340 used. Original EGO 56V battery only — aftermarket packs damage the mower. 5Ah battery healthy runtime is 50–60 minutes; under 30 means dead cells. EGO retain resale value better than any cordless brand — never overpay but expect to recoup.

pros: Zero-turn battery ride-on — silent and clean
mowers.verdict: EGO's flagship — the zero-turn battery ride-on that's actually competitive with petrol commercial kit. For estate owners, golf clubs, and large landscapers willing to invest in the future of battery groundskeeping. Domestic buyers stop reading at the price.
mowers.tip: £3000–5000 used (rare on Marketplace — these are dealer-traded). Confirm all 4 batteries are EGO genuine. Test runtime under realistic load. Service history matters — these need proper maintenance like any commercial machine.

mowers.tagline: Husqvarna mid-range petrol SP — built in Europe
pros: Husqvarna build quality and dealer support
cons: HQ engine less universal than Honda or Briggs
mowers.verdict: A solid Husqvarna walk-behind petrol. Build quality genuinely above Mountfield/Cobra at this price point. The Husqvarna HQ engine is well-engineered but parts are dealer-only — that's the trade-off vs. a Briggs-equipped rival.
mowers.tip: £200–340 used. Husqvarna chassis serial verifies dealer service history — check it. HQ engine starts cleanly cold; sluggish cold-start is a sign of varnished carb (£60 service). Drive cable and rear flap hinge are wear points; check both.

pros: Husqvarna Connect — full smartphone control + GPS theft alarm
1500m² coverage suits typical UK suburban lawns
cons: Premium price for a wired robot
mowers.verdict: The right Husqvarna for owners of typical UK suburban gardens (1000–1500m²) who don't need EPOS. Smaller and cheaper than the 430X but with the connected features and the same proven Husqvarna durability. The middle of the range and the smart middle to land on.
mowers.tip: £500–900 used. Boundary wire integrity is the make-or-break — walk every metre. Husqvarna serial verifies warranty and dealer service. App account must be reset before transfer. Charging contacts on the dock corrode — clean before judging battery life.

mowers.tagline: 4WD Husqvarna robot for steep, complex lawns
mowers.verdict: For estate owners with serious slopes. The 535 AWD does what no rival does — climbs 70% slopes that defeat every wheeled rotary. EPOS removes the boundary wire. Built to last 20 years. The most expensive domestic robot you can buy and worth it for the right buyer.
mowers.tip: £1800–3500 used. EPOS reference station MUST be included. AWD diff oil and gearbox should have service history. Confirm satellite lock at the property. Husqvarna pro-grade robots have the best longevity in the category — service stamps add £400 to fair value.

pros: Twin 24V system shares with Greenworks 24V tools
cons: Twin-battery means runtime depends on both
Greenworks 24V is a smaller ecosystem than 40V or 60V
mowers.verdict: A solid budget cordless for buyers building a Greenworks 24V tool ecosystem. The twin-battery system is the key spec — both must be healthy or runtime collapses. Outclassed by the 40V and 60V Greenworks units on raw performance.
mowers.tip: £90–160 used. Test BOTH 24V batteries individually. Mismatched packs ruin runtime. Greenworks 24V battery resale is poor — don't pay above £15 per battery used. Plastic deck cracks at the wheel mounts; flip and inspect.

pros: Runs on Ryobi 18V ONE+ — hundreds of compatible tools
Single battery design simpler than twin
mowers.verdict: The right Ryobi for someone who already owns 5+ ONE+ tools. The battery slots in cleanly and the mower handles small flat lawns. For everyone else, the EGO LM1701E or Bosch EasyRotak offers more cut for similar money.
mowers.tip: £60–120 used. Confirm a genuine Ryobi 18V battery is included (counterfeits common). 4Ah pack runs ~25 minutes. ONE+ batteries are the most counterfeited — buy from sellers with original receipts. Mower itself is well-built; battery is the entire risk.

pros: Makita LXT batteries hold the strongest resale
mowers.verdict: The right Makita for an LXT tool owner who wants self-propel. Build quality is genuinely professional. Without batteries the price is a steal; with them it's competitive with EGO. Either way, the LXT ecosystem fit is the real win.
mowers.tip: £170–280 with batteries used. Without batteries, the bare body should be £100 max. LXT batteries hold value at £45–60 each used. Self-propel cable wear is the main service item. Makita motor is bombproof — issues are almost always battery-related.

pros: PowerShare battery shared with Worx 20V tools
mowers.verdict: A perfectly fine first cordless if you already own Worx 20V tools. PowerShare is the actual reason to buy. Outclassed on raw mower performance by the bigger Worx WG779E but fits the small-lawn / cross-tool buyer cleanly.
mowers.tip: £30–80 used. PowerShare battery resale is moderate. Test both batteries individually. Worx batteries are heavily counterfeited — buy from sellers with receipts. Mower is light and easily carried, so abuse is common — flip and inspect for cracks.

pros: Gardena reliability — Husqvarna Group quality at lower price
1000m² coverage suits typical UK suburban lawns
cons: 22cm cut means slow on full perimeter
Gardena Smart app limited vs Husqvarna Connect
mowers.verdict: The Gardena Sileno Life sits between the Sileno Minimo and the Husqvarna 305 in capability and price. For 600–1000m² lawns it's the smart choice — quieter than Husqvarna, better-built than Worx Landroid. The Gardena Smart app is the weakest part.
mowers.tip: £350–650 used. Boundary wire walk-perimeter check as standard. Battery cells last about 4 years; demand a runtime test. Reset Gardena Smart account before purchase. Dock contacts gold-plated — Gardena uses better dock metal than most rivals.

mowers.tagline: Mid-range YUKA — RTK wire-free for medium lawns
mowers.verdict: The right Mammotion for buyers who want wire-free at the £2000 mark. Sits between the YUKA Mini (300m² 600m²) and LUBA 2 5000. For a 1000–1500m² lawn, it's the price-performance sweet spot in the Mammotion range.
mowers.tip: £800–1500 used (most still under warranty). RTK base mandatory. Confirm 4G/satellite lock at the buyer's property before paying. Reset the Mammotion app account before transfer. Mammotion warranty transfers cleanly via serial number — verify on purchase.

mowers.tagline: Compact Segway RTK robot for small gardens
pros: RTK no-wire setup at compact-garden price
cons: Segway brand still building UK presence
mowers.verdict: A reasonable wire-free robot for compact gardens. Bigger than the i108E with similar features. Worth checking against the Mammotion YUKA Mini at similar money — Mammotion is slightly newer and the LUBA family has better long-term reputation.
mowers.tip: £500–900 used. RTK base must be included with calibration intact. Most i105Es are recent — confirm purchase date and warranty status. Reset Segway account before purchase. Avoid units stored outside; dock electronics corrode in damp UK weather.

pros: 48cm cut + heavy steel roller for proper stripes
Atco parts continuity goes back decades
cons: Steel roller deck rusts if stored damp
Atco resale below Hayter at the same age
mowers.verdict: The Atco for buyers who want British-built stripes without paying Hayter money. Same heritage, similar build quality, slightly cheaper used. The Briggs 750EX is reliable but not Honda-level. Buy if you have a Briggs dealer locally.
mowers.tip: £200–380 used. Steel deck rust check is critical — flip and look for hole-through corrosion. Roller bearings should turn smoothly. Original Atco serial number on the deck adds £40 to fair value. Anything stored under cover is a buy.

mowers.tagline: 30cm Atco premium cordless cylinder — bowling-green stripes
pros: Cylinder cut + battery — only one of two in UK market
cons: Cylinder mowers need short fine grass
5Ah battery limits to ~40 min of cylinder mowing
mowers.verdict: For the formal-lawn obsessive who wants Allett-class cuts without going manual or petrol. The cordless cylinder is a niche but a real one — silent, clean, exquisite finish. Outsider buyers should ignore; bowling-green keepers will love it.
mowers.tip: £500–900 used. Cylinder sharpness is the entire game — test cut on paper. Re-grind is £40–60 at any cylinder mower service shop. Battery health critical (40-min runtime healthy; under 20 means dead). Atco service stamps add £100 to fair value.

pros: V-twin B&S Vanguard for serious lawns
PGC powered grass collector handles wet UK grass
cons: Niche — slower resale than John Deere
mowers.verdict: For estate owners who prefer British engineering over American. Same factory as Countax (AriensCo UK) with very similar specs and slightly different badging. The V-twin Vanguard is the engine to beat in this price class. Genuinely a forever machine.
mowers.tip: £2200–3800 used. Engine hours under 600 is good. PGC blower belt is the wear part — slipping means £40 belt and dealer fitting. Westwood/Countax service histories are interchangeable. Anything under £2200 with full service is a buy; above £4000, get a JD instead.

mowers.tagline: Mid X-series JD with rear collect
pros: JD pedigree, JD parts, JD dealer everywhere
JD resale value is the best in the category
mowers.verdict: The JD ride-on most buyers actually want — bigger than the X167R, cheaper than the X354. Mid X-series, the volume seller. Hydrostatic drive is standard, JD support is universal, resale stays strong. The default "I want a JD" answer for half-acre to acre lawns.
mowers.tip: £2500–3800 used. JD engine hours under 300 is excellent, under 600 fine. PTO clutch test critical — must engage cleanly with no slip. Hydrostatic transmission belt slip is the most common fault — test forward/reverse smoothly. JD service stamps add £400 to fair value.

pros: American Toro pedigree — pro-grade American design
60V Flex-Force shares with Toro outdoor tools
cons: Toro UK distribution thinner than US
Flex-Force 60V battery resale poor in UK
mowers.verdict: A solid American import for buyers who want a pro-grade cordless from a heritage brand. Toro is enormous in the US; the UK presence is growing but limited. Build quality is excellent. The battery ecosystem is the weakness in the UK market specifically.
mowers.tip: £250–420 used. Confirm Flex-Force battery is genuine and includes the SmartStow latch. Self-propel cable wear is common. Toro UK warranty is via dealer; check chassis number for warranty status. Battery cells degrade like any cordless — demand a runtime test.

mowers.tagline: 35cm British cylinder cordless — Allett ornamental specialist
pros: Cylinder cut + cordless = pristine ornamental finish
cons: Cylinder requires short fine grass — useless on tussocky lawns
mowers.verdict: For people who care about their lawn the way bowling-green groundsmen do. The Allett Liberty is what the Suffolk Punch wishes it were — modern, cordless, and with interchangeable cassettes that turn it into a scarifier. A specialist tool for a specialist lawn. If that's you, nothing else competes.
mowers.tip: £600–1000 used. Cylinder sharpness is non-negotiable — test cut paper. Allett re-grind service exists at their factory; £80 turnaround. Confirm cassette set is complete (cylinder is mandatory; scarifier and brush are optional but valuable). Reset Allett account if app-connected.

pros: Solid UK dealer presence in northern England
cons: AL-KO badge depreciates faster than Stihl
Mid-cut flap design clogs in wet grass
mowers.verdict: A genuinely capable German-engineered petrol at a Mountfield SP485 price. The Briggs 875EX is the engine to beat in this class. AL-KO has stronger presence in DE/AT/CH than UK, so used supply is thinner — but condition tends to be better.
mowers.tip: £170–320 used. Briggs 875EX engine hours under 100 is excellent. AL-KO chassis numbers verify dealer service. The mid-cut flap mechanism develops play; check it pivots cleanly. Less common on UK Marketplace so don't walk away from a clean one — they don't come up often.

pros: Heritage character — red and yellow stripes are iconic
cons: Drive belt and steering linkage wear with age
mowers.verdict: The Murray 11/30 was a budget garden tractor sold widely in the UK during the 1990s. Mostly red with a distinctive orange/yellow stripe across the bonnet. Out of production for years but the Briggs engine and simple gear-drive transmission make these easy to keep running. A perfectly fine first ride-on if you find a clean one.
mowers.tip: £300–650 used depending on condition. Briggs 11HP engine should fire on first or second crank — sluggish start is varnished carb (£40 fix). Test the gear-change linkage; vintage Murrays develop play in the steering box. Avoid any with welded chassis repairs or non-original wheel sizes.

mowers.tagline: Modern entry Murray ride-on — current production
cons: Murray UK distribution thin since the B&S restructure
mowers.verdict: The current entry Murray. Same cut width as the heritage 11/30 but with collection, mulching, and modern controls. Good value new but used supply is small in the UK — Mountfield 1430M and 1530H are the more obvious rivals at this price.
mowers.tip: £600–1000 used. Briggs 7150 hours under 200 is excellent. Confirm Murray UK warranty status — distribution gaps mean some units are direct-import. Murray dealer support is via Briggs & Stratton service network.

mowers.tagline: Murray battery push — modern entry cordless
mowers.verdict: Murray's budget cordless. Performs the job for a small flat lawn but the battery is the entire risk — Murray 40V packs have no resale value and limited spares. Outclassed by the Greenworks G24X2 at the same price.
mowers.tip: £45–90 used. Battery health is the entire game — demand a 20-minute runtime test. Murray 40V replacement batteries are rare; budget for a host-of-tools change if the pack dies. Plastic deck cracks at the wheel mounts.

mowers.verdict: The bigger sibling of the 11/30 — same red and yellow heritage paint, 13HP engine, 36in deck. Built when ride-ons were straightforward machines that any home mechanic could service. A good buy if you have a half-acre rough lawn and don't care about collection.
mowers.tip: £450–800 used depending on hours and condition. Briggs 13HP test as standard — cold start, no smoke, even idle. Drive belt is the most common service item; cheap to replace. Avoid any with corroded deck spindles — a common end-of-life Murray fault.

cons: Manual gearbox — less smooth than hydrostatic
Smaller deck means slow on big lawns
mowers.verdict: The right Mountfield ride-on for buyers who want the convenience of a tractor without spending Mountfield 1530H money. Manual transmission is the trade-off — but for under £1500 new it is the cheapest way into a credible UK ride-on.
mowers.tip: £550–950 used. Engine hours under 250 is good. Manual transmission needs a smooth shift through gears; sticking is usually linkage adjustment (£30 dealer fix). Steering box wears — check for play before paying.

mowers.verdict: The middle Mountfield ride-on. Bigger deck and better collection than the 827M, manual transmission keeps the price below the hydrostatic 1530H. For 1500–2500m² lawns it is the value pick.
mowers.tip: £650–1100 used. Stiga 432cc engine should idle smoothly. Same gear-linkage and steering checks as the 827M. Service history at any Mountfield dealer adds £150 to fair value.

cons: Mountfield resale below JD or Honda
mowers.verdict: Mountfield's flagship walk-behind ride-on. The Briggs Vanguard is the same engine fitted to Westwood T1600HE and many Cub Cadet models — properly built. For 3000–5000m² lawns this matches an entry John Deere on capability for two-thirds of the price.
mowers.tip: £1200–2000 used. Vanguard hours under 300 is excellent. Hydrostatic transmission belt slip under load is the classic fault — test on an incline. PTO clutch must engage cleanly. Service history through a Mountfield dealer adds £300 to fair value.

pros: Silent operation — mow at 7am without complaints
No fuel storage or oil changes
cons: Used market thin so resale uncertain
mowers.verdict: Mountfield's entry into battery ride-on territory. Silent and clean — a real differentiator if you mow early or in a noise-sensitive neighbourhood. The 2000m² single-charge limit suits typical UK suburban gardens but rules out half-acre+ lawns.
mowers.tip: £1500–2500 used (rare on Marketplace yet). Confirm the battery pack health — demand a runtime test. Mountfield 80V batteries are still under warranty on most units. Reset Mountfield account before purchase.

cons: No collection — useless on first cut
Mulching needs short dry grass to work well
mowers.verdict: For buyers who mulch religiously and never bag clippings. If you accept that a mulch-only mower needs a regular cutting schedule, this is dramatically faster than collect-and-empty. For most British lawns the bag is more useful than the mulch convenience.
mowers.tip: £120–220 used. Specialist mowers have a smaller buyer pool — bargains appear. Stiga engine same checks as any Mountfield. Mulch deck builds up grass — flip and clean before judging cut quality.

mowers.tagline: Kubota premium 40in garden tractor — Japanese build
cons: Kubota dealer network thinner than JD
mowers.verdict: For estate owners who want Japanese reliability over American badge value. Kubota garden tractors are built to a standard typical of agricultural Kubota — expect 20+ years of service if maintained. Less common on UK Marketplace than JD because owners hold them longer.
mowers.tip: £2800–4200 used. Kubota service stamps add serious value — £500+ to fair price. Engine hours under 400 excellent. Glide Steer mechanism wears — check tracking on a flat surface. Kubota chassis numbers verify warranty status.

mowers.tagline: Bigger Kubota GR — 48in deck for larger lawns
pros: Built to the same standard as the GR1600
mowers.verdict: The bigger Kubota for half-acre to acre estate lawns. Deeper deck and bigger engine than the GR1600-II — cuts in fewer passes. Worth the upgrade only if your lawn justifies it; for most domestic buyers the GR1600 is plenty.
mowers.tip: £3500–5200 used. Same Kubota service-history premium. Engine hours under 500 excellent. Hydrostatic drive belt slip under load is the most common ride-on fault — test on an incline. Glide Steer mechanism oil change is part of regular service.

mowers.tagline: Kubota diesel ride-on — the professional standard
pros: Diesel engine — 30+ year service life realistic
Used by professional groundsmen and golf clubs
mowers.verdict: For estate owners and professional groundsmen. The Kubota G23 is what cricket-club groundsmen and head gardeners actually run on. Diesel engine outlasts the rest of the machine; properly maintained these run 30 years.
mowers.tip: £5500–9000 used. Kubota diesel hours: under 1500 is excellent for a G23, under 3000 acceptable. Diesel injectors need clean fuel — ask about service history. Hydraulic system pressure test should be part of any pre-purchase inspection on a unit over £7000.

pros: Newer D902 diesel — cleaner-burning than D722
cons: Even more expensive than the G23
mowers.verdict: The current Kubota G-series flagship. Replaces the G21/G23 generation with cleaner emissions and an electronic dash. Same Kubota build quality and the same 25-30 year horizon if serviced. Domestic buyers stop reading at the price; estate owners and grounds contractors are the actual market.
mowers.tip: £6500–11000 used. New enough that hours under 1000 are common. D902 service intervals matter — confirm regular oil and filter changes. Electronic dash faults are the new failure mode — budget for a Kubota dealer diagnostic on any unit over 5 years old.

mowers.tagline: Kubota entry petrol ride-on — the smallest T-series
